dimensions.label: Transformation Purpose
dimensions.selection_semantics: Multiple purposes may coexist; downstream runtimes may choose one primary purpose plus optional secondary purposes.
dimensions.definition: Why transformation is introduced.

dimensions.label: Transformation Object
dimensions.selection_semantics: A transformation may operate on more than one object simultaneously, but one object is often primary.
dimensions.definition: What aspect of the object or task is being transformed.

dimensions.label: Transformation Approach
dimensions.selection_semantics: Hybrid transformation routes are common; multiple approach families may be combined within one stage.
dimensions.definition: How transformation is carried out.

dimensions.label: Preservation Commitment
dimensions.selection_semantics: Preservation commitments are often cumulative rather than exclusive.
dimensions.definition: What must remain preserved for the transformed route to remain valid.

dimensions.label: Optimization Role
dimensions.selection_semantics: Usually one primary role is chosen for a stage.
dimensions.definition: What optimization primarily does in the larger route.

dimensions.label: Problem Architecture
dimensions.selection_semantics: This is a grouping node that contains the stable architectural subdimensions of optimization.
dimensions.definition: How the optimization problem is organized before selecting a solver.

dimensions.label: Optimization Stance
dimensions.definition: What notion of optimality or admissible success is being sought.

dimensions.label: Feasibility Structure
dimensions.definition: How validity or admissibility is encoded.

dimensions.label: Decision Object
dimensions.selection_semantics: One object family is usually primary, but the associated Variable Type axis jointly characterizes its representation.
dimensions.definition: What is being chosen or optimized.

dimensions.label: Variable Type
dimensions.selection_semantics: Usually singular for a given formulation, though mixed/hybrid values explicitly represent combined regimes.
dimensions.definition: What variable regime characterizes the decision object.

dimensions.label: Evaluation Access
dimensions.definition: How candidate quality can be evaluated.

dimensions.label: Temporal Commitment
dimensions.definition: When decisions are committed and revised.

dimensions.label: Solution Logic
dimensions.selection_semantics: Hybrid optimization procedures often combine multiple logic families.
dimensions.definition: How the optimization process advances toward a solution.

dimensions.label: Purpose
dimensions.selection_semantics: A probabilistic route may estimate, predict, explain, evaluate, and decide in combination.
dimensions.definition: What the probabilistic route is trying to achieve.

dimensions.label: Probabilistic Formulation
dimensions.definition: How uncertainty and dependence are represented.

dimensions.label: Probability Representation
dimensions.definition: How uncertainty is formally represented.

dimensions.label: Dependence Structure
dimensions.definition: How dependence among uncertain quantities is represented.

dimensions.label: Inference Scheme
dimensions.definition: How probabilistic inference is conceptually framed and computationally realized.

dimensions.label: Inference Approach
dimensions.definition: What inferential logic governs learning or decision under uncertainty.

dimensions.label: Inference Engine
dimensions.definition: What computational engine is used to realize inference.

dimensions.label: Inversion Objective
dimensions.definition: What unknown is to be recovered and in what solution form.

dimensions.label: Inversion Target
dimensions.definition: What hidden object the inverse route aims to recover.

dimensions.label: Solution Form
dimensions.definition: What form the inverse answer takes.

dimensions.label: Forward Model Class
dimensions.definition: Container axis for the forward relation family and any optional emulation layer used in inversion.

dimensions.label: Forward Class
dimensions.definition: Primary class of forward relation used by the inverse route.

dimensions.label: Emulation Layer
dimensions.selection_semantics: Activate only when a surrogate or emulator is explicitly introduced into the inverse route.
dimensions.definition: Optional proxy model layer used when the forward model is expensive or difficult to query directly.

dimensions.label: Inversion Stabilization
dimensions.selection_semantics: Multiple stabilization families may be combined.
dimensions.definition: What stabilization burdens are introduced to make inversion well-posed enough for use.

dimensions.label: Inversion Approach
dimensions.selection_semantics: Choose the approach whose best-when conditions and limitations fit the current inverse problem.
dimensions.definition: How inversion is solved, represented as a comparison table rather than a simple taxonomy.

dimensions.label: Rule Function
dimensions.definition: What rules primarily do in the larger route.

dimensions.label: Rule Type
dimensions.definition: What validity mode a rule carries.

dimensions.label: Rule Representation
dimensions.selection_semantics: Hybrid rule encodings are common in real systems.
dimensions.definition: How knowledge is encoded into rules.

dimensions.label: Execution Pattern
dimensions.selection_semantics: A rule system may combine multiple execution patterns.
dimensions.definition: How rules are executed or consulted at runtime.

dimensions.label: Fusion Purpose
dimensions.selection_semantics: Fusion can simultaneously complete, stabilize, reconcile, and extend.
dimensions.definition: Why fusion is introduced.

dimensions.label: Fusion Locus
dimensions.selection_semantics: One or more locus layers may be active; at least one locus layer must be selected in any valid fusion route.
dimensions.definition: Where fusion operates in the larger route.

selection_constraints.definition: At least one fusion locus layer must be active in any valid fusion route.

dimensions.label: Input Layer
dimensions.selection_semantics: Optional locus; activate only if fusion occurs at the raw-source or constituent level.
dimensions.definition: Fusion at the level of raw inputs or constituents.

dimensions.label: Representation Layer
dimensions.selection_semantics: Optional locus; activate only if fusion occurs on representations or models.
dimensions.definition: Fusion at the level of encoded representations or formal models.

dimensions.label: Operational Layer
dimensions.selection_semantics: Optional locus; activate only if methods or workflows are being fused.
dimensions.definition: Fusion at the level of methods or workflows.

dimensions.label: Resolution Layer
dimensions.selection_semantics: Optional locus; activate only if outputs or explanatory frames are fused.
dimensions.definition: Fusion at the level of decisions or conceptual resolution.

dimensions.label: Integration Logic
dimensions.selection_semantics: Hybrid integration strategies are common.
dimensions.definition: How separate sources, models, methods, or stages are integrated.

dimensions.label: Simulation Purpose
dimensions.selection_semantics: A simulation route may explore, predict, calibrate, and decide in combination.
dimensions.definition: Why simulation is introduced.

dimensions.label: World Modeling Approach
dimensions.definition: How the model world is represented, accelerated, or composed.

dimensions.label: Primary Formalisms
dimensions.definition: Primary formalism used to represent the modeled world.

dimensions.label: Emulation Layer
dimensions.selection_semantics: Activate only when a surrogate or emulator is explicitly inserted for speed.
dimensions.definition: Optional fast-emulator layer used to accelerate a more expensive simulator.

dimensions.label: Composition Layer
dimensions.selection_semantics: Activate only when multiple component models are coupled or composed.
dimensions.definition: Optional coupled-model layer used when multiple models must be composed for scope.

dimensions.label: Execution Protocol
dimensions.selection_semantics: Execution protocols can be combined in one simulation program.
dimensions.definition: How the model world is interrogated or used operationally.

dimensions.label: Evolutionary Role
dimensions.definition: What evolutionary/adaptive iteration primarily does.

dimensions.label: Update Architecture
dimensions.definition: How updates are targeted, scheduled, and enacted over time.

dimensions.label: Update Target
dimensions.definition: What part of the system is being updated.

dimensions.label: Update Regime
dimensions.definition: How often and on what schedule updates occur.

dimensions.label: Update Mechanism
dimensions.selection_semantics: Multiple update mechanisms may be combined in one adaptive regime.
dimensions.definition: How the current state is revised, replaced, or extended.

dimensions.label: Feedback Signal
dimensions.selection_semantics: Multiple feedback signals may be combined.
dimensions.definition: What signal governs selection or revision.

dimensions.label: Carryover Logic
dimensions.selection_semantics: Carryover policies are often combined.
dimensions.definition: How information or structure persists across update rounds.

design_notes: This registry publishes the stable Compass-aligned semantic skeleton: major axes, stable subdimensions, and layer modules where the theoretical Compass treats a module as activatable rather than as an ordinary sibling category.
Dimensions are canonical semantic nodes. Downstream implementations may compile other operational views, but such views should remain traceable to these dimension IDs.
Associated axes are used only where the theoretical Compass structure warrants them, such as Variable Type attached to Optimization Decision Object.
